---
title: "Code & References"
---

## Notebooks

The full analysis is implemented across four Jupyter notebooks, available in the repository.

### 1. Data acquisition

[View notebook →](notebooks/01-data-acquisition.html)

Downloads LiDAR tiles from USGS, processes building footprints, extracts the pedestrian network from OSM, and loads SEPTA transit stops.

**Outputs:** `buildings_with_heights.geojson`, `edges.geojson`, `septa_stops.geojson`

---

### 2. Shade calculation

[View notebook →](notebooks/02-network-shade.html)

Rasterizes LiDAR for buildings and trees, models solar position across 8 scenarios, calculates geometric shadows, buffers street segments, and computes shade scores.

**Outputs:** `buildings_heights.tif`, `tree_heights.tif`, `network_edges_with_shade.geojson`

**Runtime:** ~2 hours

---

### 3. Routing analysis

[View notebook →](notebooks/03-routing.html)

Constructs the network graph, implements Dijkstra's algorithm with shade-weighted cost function, calculates test routes, and generates comparison metrics.

**Runtime:** ~15 minutes

---

### 4. Visualization

[View notebook →](notebooks/04-visualizations.html)

Produces static route comparison maps, shade heatmaps, and summary charts.

**Runtime:** ~10 minutes

---

## Data dictionary

### `network_edges_with_shade.geojson`

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `u` | int | Start node ID |
| `v` | int | End node ID |
| `key` | int | Edge key (for multi-edges) |
| `osmid` | int/list | OpenStreetMap ID(s) |
| `name` | str | Street name |
| `highway` | str | OSM highway type |
| `length` | float | Segment length (feet) |
| `geometry` | LineString | Segment geometry (WGS84) |
| `building_shadow_summer_midday` | float | Building shade score (0-1) |
| `tree_shadow_summer_midday` | float | Tree shade score (0-1) |
| `shade_summer_midday` | float | Combined shade score (0-1) |
| *(repeated for all 8 scenarios)* | | |

Shade score interpretation: 0.0 = full sun, 0.5 = half the pedestrian zone shaded, 1.0 = fully shaded.

---

## Software
```python
# Core geospatial
geopandas==0.14.1
shapely==2.0.2
rasterio==1.3.9
fiona==1.9.5

# Network analysis
osmnx==1.8.1
networkx==3.2.1

# LiDAR processing
laspy==2.5.1
pdal==3.3.0

# Solar modeling
pvlib==0.10.3

# Visualization
matplotlib==3.8.2
contextily==1.4.0

# General
numpy==1.26.2
pandas==2.1.4
```

**System requirements:** Python 3.10+, 16GB RAM minimum (32GB recommended for LiDAR processing), ~20GB storage.

---

## Reproducing the analysis
```bash
git clone https://github.com/kavanaraju/nadi.git
cd nadi

conda env create -f environment.yml
conda activate shade-routing

jupyter notebook
# Run notebooks in order: 01 → 02 → 03 → 04

quarto render
```

Total runtime approximately 5 hours, dominated by the shade calculation step.

---

## References

**Data**

- USGS 3D Elevation Program — https://www.usgs.gov/3d-elevation-program
- Pennsylvania Spatial Data Access (PASDA) — https://www.pasda.psu.edu
- OpenStreetMap — https://www.openstreetmap.org
- SEPTA GTFS — https://www.septa.org/developer/

**Academic**

- Labdaoui et al. (2021). Utilizing Thermal Comfort and Walking Facilities to Propose a Comfort Walkability Index. *Building and Environment*, 193.
- Middel et al. (2014). Impact of Urban Form and Design on Mid-Afternoon Microclimate. *Landscape and Urban Planning*, 122.
- Klemm et al. (2015). Street Greenery and Its Physical and Psychological Impact on Thermal Comfort. *Landscape and Urban Planning*, 138.
- Boeing (2017). OSMnx: New Methods for Acquiring, Constructing, Analyzing, and Visualizing Complex Street Networks. *Computers, Environment and Urban Systems*, 65.

**Software**

- Holmgren et al. (2018). pvlib python. *Journal of Open Source Software*, 3(29).
- Jordahl et al. (2020). geopandas/geopandas: v0.8.1.

---

**Kavana Raju** | University of Pennsylvania  
[kavana@upenn.edu](mailto:kavana@upenn.edu) | [github.com/kavanaraju/nadi](https://github.com/kavanaraju/nadi)
